_note: MERGED: 10 hand-picked PD/CC0 masterworks (lead per beat) + PD/CC0 pool harvested from Wikimedia Commons categories (round-robin dealt across beats sharing a category; extmetadata PD-filter; deduped). Each beat maps to categories of what it depicts. Curated by PlayfulProcess; all public domain / CC0; only the narration audio is AI.

images.title: Echo and Narcissus
images.credit: John William Waterhouse, “Echo and Narcissus” (1903), Walker Art Gallery, Liverpool — via Google Art Project / Wikimedia Commons
images.note: The canonical image of the whole myth — Echo watching the boy at the pool; used as the establishing wide shot over the opening (Liriope/Tiresias).

images.title: The Nymph Echo (Die Nymphe Echo / La Nymphe Écho)
images.credit: Eugène Carrière, “La Nymphe Écho” (c. 1900), Bavarian State Painting Collections — Wikimedia Commons
images.note: Echo as a lone, vapour-soft figure — anchors 'Echo introduced'.

images.title: A Bather (Echo)
images.credit: Jean-Jacques Henner, “A Bather (Echo)”, The Metropolitan Museum of Art (Open Access, CC0) — Wikimedia Commons
images.license: CC0 1.0 Public Domain Dedication (The Met Open Access; Henner d. 1905)
images.note: A solitary fading figure for 'Echo wastes to a voice'.

images.title: Narcissus at the Well (Metamorphoseon ... Ovidianarum, pl. 28)
images.credit: Antonio Tempesta, “Narcissus at the Well”, from the Ovid Metamorphoses etchings (1606), The Metropolitan Museum of Art (Open Access, obj. 401131)
images.license: Public domain (Met flags isPublicDomain:true; Tempesta d. 1630)
images.note: Period Ovid-edition engraving of the boy at the unsullied fountain.

images.title: Narcissus
images.credit: Caravaggio, “Narcissus” (c. 1594-1596), Galleria Nazionale d'Arte Antica, Rome — Wikimedia Commons
images.note: The definitive reflection image — anchors 'He falls in love with his reflection'.

images.title: Narcissus (Narciso)
images.credit: Jan Cossiers, “Narciso” (c. 1650), Museo Nacional del Prado, Madrid — Wikimedia Commons
images.note: A second take on the reflection, to rotate against the Caravaggio across the long pool scene.

images.title: Narcissus
images.credit: Gyula Benczúr, “Narcissus” (1881) — Wikimedia Commons (Fine Arts in Hungary)
images.note: The youth absorbed in himself — anchors 'Loving himself, sought and seeking'.

images.title: Narcisse
images.credit: Nicolas-Bernard Lépicié, “Narcisse” (c. 1771) — Wikimedia Commons
images.note: Anchors 'The narrator's warning' / the boy fixed at the water.

images.title: Echo and Narcissus
images.credit: Nicolas Poussin, “Echo and Narcissus” (c. 1630), Musée du Louvre, Paris — via Web Gallery of Art / Wikimedia Commons
images.note: Narcissus dead/dying with grieving Echo and the first flower — anchors the death.

images.title: Narcissus in Florem (Ovid Metamorphoses, Book 3, pl. 33)
images.credit: Johann Wilhelm Baur, “Narcissus in Florem”, pl. 33 of the 1703 Nuremberg Ovid (designs c. 1641), The Ovid Project, University of Vermont (Hope Greenberg)
images.license: Public domain (Baur d. 1642; 1703 print PD by age)
images.note: The transformation into the flower — closes the stream.
